prompt: A small team is choosing between writing software in TypeScript or Python for a new internal data tool. Walk through how you would help them decide, in prose (not a bulleted list).
response:
The first thing I would do is sit with the team in the room where they actually work and listen for what is not being said. Choosing a language is rarely about the language itself; it is about time, fear, ambition, and the ghosts of projects past. Someone will mention “type safety” with a half-remembered war story from a JavaScript codebase that grew feral; someone else will murmur “data ecosystem” while thinking of the afternoon last month when a single Pandas call did in ten minutes what had taken the Scala squad three sprints. I would let those fragments settle, because until the emotional ledger is visible the technical ledger cannot be trusted.

When the stories have thinned out, I would ask them to walk me through the life of a single hypothetical data record from birth to archive. Which human being creates it, which cron job kisses it awake at 3 a.m., which dashboard will betray it next quarter. As they speak, I would sketch the path on a whiteboard, annotating wherever the data changes shape: CSV upload, JSON squirt from a partner API, the Parquet file that will one day be subpoenaed by Legal. The places where shape matters are the places where TypeScript’s strict compiler is a quiet colleague who never sleeps; the places where shape dissolves into exploratory munging are the places where Python’s REPL is a campfire you can circle in minutes. Watching their eyes flick toward either column of the board usually tells you which comfort zone is really calling the shots.

Next I would ask them to inventory the muscles they already have. Not résumé bullets, but the calluses earned from debugging at 2 a.m. If two of the three maintainers reach for Python when no one is looking—because they trust its tracebacks, because their muscle memory lives in Jupyter—then adopting TypeScript means paying for two educations while the backlog glares. Conversely, if the team already speaks React and GraphQL with fluency, TypeScript is not a new country; it is just the next room in the same house. I have seen a squad convince themselves they should “learn data science properly” and elect Python, only to discover that their institutional memory lived in a monorepo full of generics and mapped types; six months later they were bilingual and bitter.

Then comes the moment when we must quantify the invisible: how much failure can the company afford in public? Internal tools are supposedly safe to break, yet the demo that dies in the executive Zoom becomes external instantly. I would ask them to write, on a single index card, the worst outage they are willing to explain to the CFO. If the card reads “a few malformed rows in a Tuesday morning CSV,” Python’s fast, loose iteration is probably an acceptable risk. If the card reads “we accidentally duplicated every primary key and Finance published the wrong revenue number to the street,” the card is arguing for TypeScript’s compile-time seat belts—even if the first sprint feels like assembling IKEA furniture with mittens.

We would also have to stalk the corridors of the company and see what code is already alive in the dark. If the data platform team maintains a Scala ingestion service, the JVM artifacts probably already expose Thrift or Avro schemas; TypeScript can consume those schemas at build time and guarantee that the front-end dialogues match the back-end contracts. If the machine-learning group has a garden of Python notebooks that produce the features this new tool must consume, then staying in Python means the tool can import those modules directly instead of re-implementing feature logic and praying the two codebases drift no further apart than estranged siblings.

Performance appears next, but I would reframe it. For an internal tool, human latency usually dwarfs machine latency: the thirty seconds a researcher spends waiting for a query are thirty seconds she might open Reddit and lose flow. Python’s GIL is rarely the villain here; the villain is a scatter-brained query planner or a blob of JSON being hydrated into a thousand objects one attribute at a time. Still, if the envisioned workload is “ingest a million events per second and serve sub-second aggregations,” the team needs to know that they will eventually rewrite the hot path in Rust or Go regardless of whether the orchestration layer is Python or TypeScript. Naming that future rewrite early prevents the language choice from becoming a religious tattoo.

Finally, I would ask them to imagine the team they will be six hires from now. If the company’s leadership believes the next ten data scientists will be Python natives recruited from academia, then choosing TypeScript today means every new hire must cross a membrane; the cost is paid in onboarding weeks and muted creativity. If instead the company is staffing up on product engineers who already maintain a React front-end, TypeScript becomes the path of least resistance. The mistake is to choose for today’s three people instead of for the constellation that will own the pager rotation eighteen months hence.

When the conversation has circled back to itself twice and the whiteboard is a palimpsest of hopes and anxieties, I would tear off the top sheet, hand it to the quietest member, and ask her to summarize what she heard. Almost always, one language has become the protagonist in her story. The team will feel the tilt; someone will laugh nervously and say, “So we’re really doing this.” At that point the decision is made; the rest is just writing the ADR and scheduling the first commit.
